hook: The five-domed Orthodox cathedral that has anchored Praga's skyline since the days of the Tsars.
facts: Built 1867-1869 in a Byzantine-Russian style on a Greek-cross plan, topped with five domes, to serve the Russian community of right-bank Warsaw.
Designed by architect Mikołaj (Nikolai) Syczew; consecrated 29 June 1867.
Holds the status of metropolitan sobór (cathedral) since 1921 and is the principal church of the Polish Autocephalous Orthodox Church.
The upper church centres on a gilded three-tier iconostasis with icons by Wasilij Wasiliew; a cycle of 27 frescoes was painted by Siergiej Winogradow.
Stands at al. Solidarności 52, by the corner with ul. Targowa.

sources.note: history, architect, iconostasis and fresco painters
Praga heritage entry corroborating address and build dates

w: The five-domed Orthodox cathedral that has crowned Praga's skyline since the 1860s, built for the Tsar's right-bank subjects. Inside, a gilded three-tier iconostasis and a cycle of 27 frescoes glow in the half-dark. The seat of Poland's Orthodox Church since 1921. A piece of old Russia, by the Targowa tram lines.

hook: The brick basilica on Kawęczyńska that Pope Pius XI is said to have called "the most beautiful church in Poland."
facts: Built 1907-1923 to a design by Łukasz Wolski, founded by Prince Michał Piotr Radziwiłł at the urging of his wife Maria.
Pope Pius XI, who as nuncio Achille Ratti had followed its construction, granted it the title of minor basilica on 23 August 1923.
Its 24 granite columns were originally cut for Rome's Basilica of St Paul Outside the Walls but rejected there as too short.
The lower church can hold roughly 10,000 people; the parish has been run by the Salesians.
Stands at ul. Kawęczyńska 53 in industrial Praga; made a sanctuary of the Warsaw-Praga diocese in 2018.
legend: Pius XI reportedly called it "La più bella chiesa in Polonia" — the most beautiful church in Poland.

sources.note: build years, architect, columns, Pius XI quote, coordinates
parish history corroborating founders and Salesian custody

w: A vast brick basilica on industrial Kawęczyńska that Pius XI is said to have called the most beautiful church in Poland — he had watched it rise as papal nuncio. Its 24 granite columns were cut for St Paul's in Rome and rejected there as too short. The lower church holds ten thousand. Praga's improbable cathedral of the workers.

hook: A 1930s votive church for the 1920 "Miracle on the Vistula," raised where Praga's oldest church once stood.
facts: Built 1929-1931 to a design by Konstanty Sylwin Jakimowicz as a votive offering for the Polish victory in the 1920 Battle of Warsaw.
Erected near the former royal electoral field, on the site where Praga's oldest church stood until the 18th century, later a cemetery chapel that became a parish church in 1917.
Designed in a modernist idiom with Romanesque and Gothic elements; entered in the register of monuments in 1979.
Co-cathedral (konkatedra) of the Warsaw-Praga diocese since 1992 and Sanctuary of Our Lady of Victory, the diocese's patroness.
A wooden belfry of 1817 in the grounds — long the oldest wooden structure in Praga — burned down in 2025.
Stands at ul. Grochowska 365.

sources.note: build years, architect, electoral-field site, belfry, coordinates
diocese page corroborating co-cathedral status and address

w: A votive church for the Miracle on the Vistula — Poland's near-impossible defeat of the Red Army in 1920 — raised in the early 1930s where Praga's oldest church once stood. Modernist, with Romanesque and Gothic notes. Its 1817 wooden belfry, long Praga's oldest, burned in 2025. Thanks, in brick, for a miracle.

hook: A 900-metre oxbow lake in Skaryszewski Park — a severed arm of the old Vistula with a wartime grave on its bed.
facts: An elongated former Vistula oxbow, about 8 hectares and 900 m long, that kept a direct link to the river until the early 20th century.
Lies within Park Skaryszewski in Praga-Południe; water depth runs 2-5 m.
On 14 August 1944 German flak downed a RAF 178 Squadron Liberator that crashed into the lake; most of the crew died, with only Henry Leyne surviving, wounded and captured.
A memorial stone to the airmen was unveiled lakeside in 1988 by Margaret Thatcher.
Its western end now forms Praga Port, linked to Gocławskie Lake and the park's ponds.

sources.note: dimensions, oxbow origin, Liberator crash, Thatcher memorial, coordinates
local heritage page corroborating lake and park setting

w: A 900-metre oxbow lake in Skaryszewski Park, a severed arm of the old Vistula — and a war grave on its bed. In August 1944 German flak downed an RAF Liberator running supplies to the Uprising; it plunged in, the crew all but one killed. Margaret Thatcher unveiled their memorial stone here in 1988. Still water, long memory.

hook: Poland's oldest puppet theatre, working out of a pre-war Jewish school on Jagiellońska.
facts: Founded in 1928 (taking the name Baj in 1930) by the socialist Robotnicze Towarzystwo Przyjaciół Dzieci — the oldest puppet theatre in Poland.
Has occupied the former Michał Bergson Educational Building of the Warsaw Jewish Community at ul. Jagiellońska 28 since the 1950s.
In 2014 it received the Gold Medal Gloria Artis "Zasłużony Kulturze" — the only children's theatre so honoured.
A full modernisation of the Jagiellońska building was completed in 2020.
Shares the historic building with kindergarten no. 183.

sources.note: founding 1928, oldest puppet theatre, Bergson building, coordinates

w: Poland's oldest puppet theatre, founded by socialist educators in 1928 and working since the 1950s from a pre-war Jewish school on Jagiellońska. The only children's theatre to win Poland's Gloria Artis gold medal. Puppets for the city's young, in a building with a past. Strings pulled here for nearly a century.

hook: Saska Kępa's landmark church — a copper-roofed modernist hall finished by a poet-priest's contemporaries.
facts: Begun before WWII at ul. Nobla 16; the original wooden church burned in 1939 and the brick one was largely raised in 1946-1952.
Designed by Józef Łowiński and, post-war, Jan Bogusławski, in modernist brick, concrete and copper sheeting.
Consecrated in 1980 by Cardinal Stefan Wyszyński; entered in the register of monuments in 1979.
Poet-priest Jan Twardowski served here as vicar in 1957-1958; a stone monument to him was placed in 2015.
Holds a 51-voice, four-manual organ.

sources.note: architects, build years, Twardowski, organ, coordinates
local heritage page corroborating Saska Kępa location and history

w: Saska Kępa's landmark church — a modernist hall of brick, concrete and green copper, begun before the war, burned in 1939, and raised again through the 1940s and '50s. The poet-priest Jan Twardowski served here as a young vicar; a stone remembers him. A 51-voice organ fills it. Quiet modernism on the leafy Kępa.

hook: Praga's red-brick vodka monopoly, reborn as a square ringed by the old distillery halls.
facts: The Warszawska Wytwórnia Wódek "Koneser" was founded in 1897 by the Polish-Russian Warsaw Society for the Purification and Sale of Spirits.
Between the wars it made iconic brands including Wyborowa and Luksusowa; spirit production on the site continued until 2002 and the works closed in 2007.
Revitalised 2014-2018 into a mixed-use quarter of restored factory halls, flats, offices and culture around the half-hectare Plac Konesera.
Spans the block bounded by Ząbkowska, Nieporęcka, Białostocka and Markowska streets (plac Konesera 1-10).
The Polish Vodka Museum, opened here in June 2018, is just one tenant of the wider complex.

sources.note: official site distinguishing the complex from the Vodka Museum

w: Praga's red-brick vodka works, where Wyborowa and Luksusowa were distilled from 1897 until the stills fell silent in 2002 — reborn since as a square ringed by the restored factory halls, flats and bars. The Vodka Museum is only one tenant now. Industry turned to nightlife, the old brick kept. Plac Konesera, after the spirits.

hook: The oldest house in Praga-Północ, with a wooden gallery hidden in its courtyard on Ząbkowska.
facts: A single-storey brick tenement at the corner of Ząbkowska 13 and Brzeska, reckoned the oldest building in Praga-Północ.
Built around 1869-1870 to a design by Antoni Siekiera in a neoclassical style, surviving the great Praga fire of June 1868 era rebuild in brick.
Its courtyard keeps a picturesque wooden gallery (galeryjka), faithfully reconstructed, and the front shutters were restored.
Added to the register of monuments in July 2010 for its rare surviving form.
For over two decades it has housed the "Stara Praga" antiques gallery.

sources.note: oldest building in Praga-Północ, Siekiera design, 1870 rebuild, monument listing
Ząbkowska 13 / Brzeska corner location, wooden gallery, corroborates coordinates

w: The oldest house in Praga-Północ, a low brick tenement of around 1870 on the Ząbkowska corner — and in its courtyard, a picturesque wooden gallery of the kind that once laced the district, lovingly rebuilt. Listed in 2010 for its rarity; an antiques gallery keeps it now. A survivor of old, wooden Praga, in brick.

n: Królikarnia (Pałac i Muzeum Rzeźby im. Xawerego Dunikowskiego)
hook: A rotunda palace named for the Saxon-era rabbit hunts that once ran here, now Poland's national museum of sculpture wrapped around a wooded ravine of the Vistula escarpment.
facts: Built in the 1780s for Karol Tomatis (Charles de Valery), to a design by Dominik Merlini, the royal architect of King Stanisław August.
The name 'Królikarnia' (rabbit warren) comes from the Saxon period, when a game park here was used for fashionable rabbit hunts.
Gutted by fire in 1879; the Pusłowski family's reconstruction is cited as a pioneering act of Polish conservation practice.
Destroyed again in 1939 and during the 1944 Warsaw Uprising; rebuilt 1948–1965 to house the sculpture of Xawery Dunikowski, finished a year after his death.
Today it is a branch of the National Museum in Warsaw holding the country's largest collection of historic and contemporary sculpture.
w: A rotunda palace of the 1780s named for the rabbit hunts the Saxon kings once held here — now Poland's national museum of sculpture, set above a wooded ravine of the Vistula escarpment. Burned, rebuilt, destroyed again in 1944, raised once more to hold Xawery Dunikowski's work. The largest collection of sculpture in the country.

n: Park Morskie Oko i Pałac Szustra
hook: A romantic sentimental garden draped over the Vistula escarpment, with a neo-Gothic palace, a 'Gothic Cottage' dovecote and a Moorish folly hidden among its ponds.
facts: Laid out 1772–1774 as a sentimental landscape garden for Princess Izabela Lubomirska, with input from architect Szymon Bogumił Zug.
Efraim Szreger (Schroeger) raised a square Classicist villa on the upper terrace for Lubomirska in the same period.
In 1845 Franciszek Szuster turned the grounds into a fashionable summer resort; the lower park became a popular amusement spot called 'Promenada'.
The palace burned down completely during the 1944 Warsaw Uprising and was rebuilt in neo-Gothic form in 1962–1965 under Jerzy Brabander.
The park complex still contains the Szuster Mausoleum, the 'Gothic Cottage' (Gołębnik / dovecote) and a Moorish-style pavilion.
w: A romantic garden tumbling down the escarpment, laid out for Princess Lubomirska in the 1770s — ponds, a neo-Gothic palace, a Moorish folly and a 'Gothic Cottage' dovecote tucked among the trees. By the 1840s it had become a fashionable summer resort. The palace burned in 1944 and rose again. A sentimental landscape, still dreaming.

hook: A 1880s Tsarist brick fort on the swampy edge of Sadyba, twice turned into a Polish redoubt — in 1939 and again in the 1944 Uprising — now a military-technology museum.
facts: Built around 1883 as part of the outer ring of the Warsaw Fortress, on a pentagonal plan with a wet moat suited to the marshy ground.
Most of its combat structures were blown up around 1913 after the 1909 decision to liquidate the fortress, leaving the broken-outline barracks.
In September 1939 it was the scene of a heroic defence by Polish volunteer companies before falling to the Germans on 26 September.
Taken on 7 August 1944 by soldiers under Lt. Dr Czesław Szczubełek, it again served as an insurgent base for sorties during the Warsaw Uprising.
The eastern part became a park named after Czesław Szczubełek in 1993; the fort now houses the Museum of Polish Military Technology (and formerly the Katyń Museum until 2009).
w: An 1880s Tsarist brick fort on the marshy edge of Sadyba, its moat once wet against the swamp — twice made a Polish redoubt, defended fiercely in 1939 and seized again by insurgents in 1944. The Russians had blown up its guns decades before. It holds a museum of military technology now. A fortress fought over again and again.

hook: An ordinary fruit-and-veg market hall where, in August 1944, tens of thousands of Ochota's civilians were herded into one of the Uprising's most notorious sites of murder and mass rape.
facts: A pre-war vegetable market nicknamed 'Zieleniak', on the site of today's Banacha market at Grójecka 95 (corner of Opaczewska).
From 4 to 20 August 1944 it served as a collection point during the 'Pacyfikacja Ochoty', run by the SS-RONA brigade of Bronisław Kamiński.
Around 60,000 residents of Ochota and other districts passed through; the death toll at Zieleniak is estimated at roughly 1,000, including at least 300 murdered by RONA soldiers.
Civilians were marched on to Warszawa Zachodnia station and deported to the Dulag 121 transit camp in Pruszków.
A Karol Tchorek memorial plaque marks the spot; on 4 August 2022 a second plaque was added commemorating the women and girls raped here by RONA troops.
w: An ordinary fruit-and-vegetable market — and, for two weeks in August 1944, one of the Uprising's worst killing grounds. As Ochota was 'pacified,' 60,000 civilians were driven into the Zieleniak by Kamiński's RONA brigade, who murdered, looted and raped; about a thousand died. Two plaques remember them. The market that became a hell.

hook: The first church built from scratch in postwar Warsaw — a stark modernist box on the escarpment, its concrete lacework nodding to Auguste Perret.
facts: Stands at Puławska 95 and is the first church erected from the ground up in Warsaw after World War II.
Designed by architect Władysław Pieńkowski, with interiors by Tadeusz Wojciechowski; conceived in the Socialist Realist era but programmatically modernist.
Construction began in August 1950; the eastern part was finished in 1959 and the whole church completed in 1966.
Its rectangular, geometric mass rises over the slope of the escarpment for added monumentality, its facade rhythmically divided by vertical fins.
The reinforced-concrete openwork detail favoured by Pieńkowski is inspired by the work of French concrete master Auguste Perret.
w: The first church built from scratch in postwar Warsaw — a stark modernist box on the Puławska escarpment, begun in 1950 and finished in 1966, its facade ribbed with vertical fins and laced with openwork concrete in homage to Auguste Perret. Conceived in the socialist-realist years, but defiantly modern. Faith, cast in raw concrete.

hook: A garden-city enclave of nearly a hundred manor-style villas, built in the 1920s on parcelled military land between Filtry and Pole Mokotowskie.
facts: Built 1922 onward over about four years, on military land parcelled out between Nowowiejska and Pole Mokotowskie.
Around 100 villas were raised, designed by architects including Antoni Dygat, Marian Kontkiewicz, Adam Paprocki and Józef Referowski.
The earliest houses leaned Baroque; later villas by Dygat, Konstanty Sylwin Jakimowicz and Kontkiewicz evoked Polish manor houses with columned porticoes and tall tiled roofs.
The colony is bounded by Wawelska (Trasa Łazienkowska), Sędziowska, Nowowiejska and Krzywicki streets, plus a multi-storey block at Filtrowa 30.
It survives as a leafy 'garden city' enclave of quiet streets and gardens that once housed many notable residents.
w: A garden-city enclave of nearly a hundred villas, built through the 1920s on parcelled army land between Filtry and Pole Mokotowskie — some Baroque, most evoking the Polish manor house, with columned porches and tall tiled roofs. Quiet leafy streets that have housed the great and good ever since. The countryside, built into the city.

hook: The crumbling concrete grandstand of Warsaw's old athletics stadium — once home to Poland's first tartan track, left to sprout weeds for years on the edge of Pole Mokotowskie.
facts: The stadium at Wawelska 5 opened in 1953, built on grounds where Warszawianka had a stadium from 1934 before Skra took them over in 1946.
In 1969 it received Poland's first tartan running track, a landmark of postwar sports-construction technique.
Run into ruin by the RKS Skra club, the athletics stadium was closed in 2019 by building inspectors as a hazard, and its stands began to overgrow.

The city reclaimed the grounds; Phase 1 modernisation began January 2024 and the new Ośrodek Wawelska training complex opened in April 2025, while the historic main grandstand remained a closed ruin awaiting later phases.
w: The ghost grandstand of Skra's old athletics stadium, off Pole Mokotowskie — once home to Poland's first tartan track, laid in 1969. The club ran it into the ground; inspectors shut it in 2019, and birch and weeds took the terraces. A new complex opened alongside in 2025, but the great stand still moulders. A ruin in the city.

hook: A new district built on the bones of Warsaw's mightiest brewery, where Haberbusch & Schiele once brewed for an empire.
facts: Stands on the site of the Haberbusch, Schiele & Klawe brewery, founded after Błażej Haberbusch and Konstanty Schiele bought Jan Schöffer's Krochmalna-Street brewery at auction.
By the end of the 19th century it had grown into the largest brewery in the entire Kingdom of Poland (Królestwo Polskie).
Nationalised after WWII, it operated as the state 'Browary Warszawskie' from 1948 until 2004 from the Grzybowska 58/70 site.
Echo Investment bought the property in 2014 and built the present multi-use Browary Warszawskie complex (offices, flats, food court) preserving brewery buildings.
A new street on the site was officially named 'Haberbuscha i Schiele' to honour the founders.
legend: The brewers' name became a Warsaw beer byword, and locals still talk of the lost 'Haberbusch' as the city's defining lager.
w: A new quarter risen on the bones of Warsaw's mightiest brewery — Haberbusch & Schiele, the largest in the whole Kingdom of Poland, brewing on Grzybowska until the state taps closed in 2004. Offices, flats and a food court fill it now, the old brewery buildings kept, a street named for the founders. Beer's ghost, redeveloped.

hook: Inside Wola's vast 19th-century gasworks, the apparatus hall that once lit Warsaw is now a museum of gas and flame.
facts: Housed in the former Warsaw Gas Plant ('gazownia na Czystem') on ul. Kasprzaka, founded 1888 on the former Dworska Street near the Wola railway sidings.
At its creation it was the largest gas plant on Polish lands and became the city's main gas producer in the 20th century.
Closed during WWII, reopened in 1945 and produced gas from coal until 1970.
The Museum of the Gas Industry was organised in 1978 in the oldest surviving building, the former apparatus and compression hall.
The museum building was fully restored during a revitalisation of the gasworks complex completed in 2022.
w: Inside Wola's huge 19th-century gasworks, the hall that once made the gas to light Warsaw is now a museum of gas and flame. Built in 1888 as the largest on Polish soil, it burned coal into light until 1970; the museum opened in its oldest building in 1978, restored when the works were reborn in 2022. Industry, lights still on.

hook: A housing estate raised literally on the rubble of the Warsaw Ghetto — an 'estate-monument' meant to be left bare red brick as a wound.
facts: Designed by architect Bohdan Lachert as an 'osiedle-pomnik' (estate-monument) commemorating the destroyed Jewish district and the Ghetto.
Built 1949–1956 on the ruins of the Northern District; terraces and embankments were shaped from war rubble, and walls used 'gruzobeton' (rubble-concrete) ground on site from debris and bricks.
Lachert wanted the blocks left unplastered so the reddish brick rubble would evoke the dead — of the Ghetto, its liquidation and the Uprising.
After 1949 the modernist design was pushed toward Socialist Realism, and Lachert was ordered to plaster the blocks, obscuring the original memorial meaning.
The estate (Muranów Południowy) was entered into the register of monuments on 21 December 2023, spanning parts of Śródmieście and Wola between al. Solidarności and ul. Andersa.
legend: Residents speak of an 'underground Muranów' — cellars and foundations resting on compacted ghetto debris, so that people live atop a buried city.
w: Bohdan Lachert built this estate, deliberately, from the rubble of the Warsaw Ghetto — its terraces shaped from war debris, its walls cast in concrete ground from the ruins. He wanted the raw red brick left bare, a monument to the dead. The state ordered it plastered over instead. An estate-monument, its meaning hidden under render.

hook: A Tsarist artillery fort buried in a Żoliborz park, once a hiding place for Poland's oldest archives, now a house of culture.
facts: Built 1849–1851 as Fort Siergieja, an outer work of the Warsaw Citadel; originally a brick artillery tower in a dry moat, expanded 1864–1874 with a lunette-shaped earthwork.
Renamed Fort Sokolnickiego in 1921 after General Michał Sokolnicki, the Polish commander and military engineer.
Folded into the new Stefan Żeromski Park between the wars, its ramparts incorporated into the park layout.
From April 1944 it hid roughly 20% of the holdings of the Central Archives of Historical Records (AGAD).
It served as a field hospital during the 1944 Warsaw Uprising and since 2021 houses the Żoliborz Cultural Centre (Żoliborski Dom Kultury).
w: A Tsarist artillery fort sunk in a Żoliborz park, its ramparts folded into the lawns between the wars — and a hiding place, in 1944, for a fifth of Poland's oldest historical archives. A field hospital during the Uprising; a house of culture now. The old Citadel outwork, gone gentle. Guns to galleries.

hook: Once voted the most beautiful new metro station in the world — an underground ellipse that glows a different colour by the hour.
facts: Opened on 8 April 2005 beneath Żoliborz's central Plac Wilsona, on Warsaw Metro Line 1.
Designed by architect Andrzej M. Chołdzyński (AMC studio).
Its signature is an elliptical dome-skylight ('lampion') that lights the station in shifting colours through the day.
On 7 April 2008 it was named the world's most beautiful metro station built in recent years at the Metrorail conference in Copenhagen, beating Vancouver, Madrid and Copenhagen.
In February 2014 CNN listed it among the 12 most impressive metro stations in Europe.
w: Once voted the most beautiful new metro station in the world — a Żoliborz platform under an elliptical skylight that glows a different colour through the day. Andrzej Chołdzyński designed it; in 2008 it beat Madrid and Copenhagen for the title. Most metro stops you leave; this one rewards arriving early. Underground, lit like a lantern.

hook: The last great splinter of the medieval Mazovian Forest, saved because hermits demanded silence around their monastery.
facts: The largest surviving remnant of the medieval Mazovian Forest (Puszcza Mazowiecka) within Warsaw, on the Vistula escarpment.
Its survival is owed to the Camaldolese hermitage founded nearby in 1639, whose rule of isolation kept the natural forest from being cleared as the city grew.
Declared a nature reserve on 10 March 1973, after post-war crowds at a 'Park of Culture' here damaged the woodland.
Conservation aims to restore native Mazovian-forest species and remove introduced black locust and red oak.
Designated trails were opened to the public in 2016, with priority given to pedestrians on the walking-cycling routes.
w: The last great splinter of the medieval Mazovian Forest left inside Warsaw — and it survives because hermits demanded silence. The Camaldolese monastery founded here in 1639 kept the woods uncleared as the city spread. A nature reserve since 1973, oaks and hornbeam where the city thins out. Old-growth, saved by a vow of quiet.

hook: A baroque hermitage church that gave a whole Warsaw district its name — for the white-robed 'bielani' monks who once kept silence here.
facts: The Camaldolese came to the hill (Góra Polkowa) above the Vistula in 1639 on an endowment from King Władysław IV Waza, settling on the edge of the Mazovian Forest.
The Church of the Immaculate Conception was built 1669–1710 in late Baroque on an elongated-octagon plan with two chapels, with finishing work continuing for decades.
Thirteen hermitages were raised near the church; in one King John Casimir made retreats.
The church holds the heart of King Michał Korybut Wiśniowiecki and his mother; the grave of scholar Stanisław Staszic lies nearby.
The monks wore white habits and were popularly called 'bielani' — the nickname that gave the left-bank district of Bielany its name.
legend: Tradition holds that the order's vow of solitude is the true reason the surrounding forest was never felled, leaving Warsaw its wild green edge.
w: The baroque hermitage church that named a whole district — for the white-robed 'bielani,' the silent Camaldolese monks King Władysław IV settled on this Vistula hill in 1639. Thirteen hermitages once stood around it; a king made his retreats in one. It holds a royal heart, and Staszic lies nearby. Bielany, from the monks in white.

hook: Szymon Bogumił Zug's vast Lutheran rotunda — a Warsaw take on the Pantheon whose dome was once the highest lookout in the city.
facts: Built 1777–1781 to a design by Szymon Bogumił Zug after Lutherans received a royal privilege from King Stanisław August Poniatowski in 1777.
A Classicist rotunda about 33.4 m in diameter, crowned by a dome and lantern reaching roughly 58 m; on completion it was the tallest building in Warsaw.
The gallery around the lantern was long the city's highest viewpoint; photographer Karol Beyer shot one of the world's first 360° panoramas of Warsaw from it in 1857–1858.
Incendiary bombs gutted the church on 16 September 1939 and only burnt walls survived the Warsaw Uprising; rebuilt and returned to the Evangelical parish in 1956.
Modelled in part on the Roman Pantheon and built on a Greek-cross plan with a central rotunda.
w: A Lutheran cemetery of 1792, laid out by the architect Zug on a neat grid of numbered quarters — resting place of Warsaw's Protestant burghers: the lexicographer Linde, the painter Gerson, Emil Wedel of the chocolate dynasty. The Halpert chapel is its landmark; hard fighting crossed it in 1944. Old Warsaw, in rows.

hook: A miniature Pantheon on plac Trzech Krzyży, raised because a tsar wanted a triumphal gate and got a church instead.
facts: Built 1818–1825 to a Classicist design by Chrystian Piotr Aigner, consecrated 18 June 1826.
Erected in honour of Tsar Alexander I, who declined a planned triumphal arch on his route from Wilanów and asked for a church for Poles instead.
Its domed, columned form is directly inspired by the Roman Pantheon and stands at the centre of plac Trzech Krzyży.
Destroyed by German bombing in September 1944 and reconstructed in its original form in the 1950s.
Celebrated its 200th anniversary in the 2020s as one of Warsaw's best-known churches.
w: A miniature Pantheon on plac Trzech Krzyży, built because a tsar turned down a triumphal arch and asked for a church for the Poles instead. Aigner gave Alexander I his domed, columned classicism in the 1820s. German bombs flattened it in 1944; it was rebuilt to the old design. An emperor's polite refusal, in stone.

hook: Sobieski's thank-you for the relief of Vienna — and the church where his embalmed heart still rests beside the entrails of a Saxon king.
facts: King Jan III Sobieski laid the cornerstone on 16 July 1683 as a votive offering for his victory over the Turks at Vienna; the Capuchins owe their presence in Poland to him.
The king's embalmed heart, sealed in a metal casket, was placed in the church's catacombs after his death in 1696; his body was later moved to Wawel in 1733 but the heart stayed.
In 1830 a marble sarcophagus designed by Henryk Marconi was made for the heart in the Royal Chapel (Kaplica Królewska).
Opposite Sobieski's heart, a second sarcophagus holds the entrails of King August II the Strong.
A Baroque church on ul. Miodowa run by the Capuchin friars.
w: King Sobieski laid this church's first stone in 1683 to thank God for breaking the Turks at Vienna — and his embalmed heart still rests in its Royal Chapel. His body went to Wawel; the heart stayed. Across from it lies a stranger relic: the entrails of the Saxon king August the Strong. Two kings, in pieces, on Miodowa.

hook: A garrison cathedral that has been a Piarist church, a tsarist Orthodox cerkiew and the home of the Polish Army's chaplaincy — all on the same walls.
facts: Built in stone for the Piarists after the earlier wooden church burned in the 1656 battle with the Swedes; consecrated in 1701 to Our Lady of Victory and Saints Primus and Felician (architect Józef Fontana).
Governor Ivan Paskiewicz handed it to the Russian Orthodox Church in 1834; it was rebuilt for Orthodox liturgy in 1835–1837.
Returned to Catholics in 1919 and made a garrison church for Polish soldiers.
Roughly 40% destroyed in WWII and rebuilt 1946–1960 to Leon Marek Suzin's design.
Became the Field Cathedral of the Polish Army when John Paul II restored the Military Ordinariate in 1991.
w: One set of walls, three faiths' worth of history: a Piarist church of 1701, handed to the Russian Orthodox by a tsarist governor in 1834, returned to Catholics in 1919 and made the Polish Army's field cathedral in 1991. An anchor and a propeller hang inside now. Rebuilt after the war. The chaplaincy's home, much converted.

hook: A queen's votive dome over the New Town square that became a mass grave when it collapsed on nuns and refugees in 1944.
facts: Founded by Queen Maria Kazimiera Sobieska as a votive offering for the victory at Vienna; she brought the Sacramentine nuns from France in 1687–1688.
Built 1688–1692 on a Greek-cross plan to a design by Tylman van Gameren, with the king's architect Augustyn Locci; the dome's lantern is crowned with a royal crown.
The main altar, after modified Tylman plans, was consecrated in 1715.
On 31 August 1944 the church was destroyed and around 1,000 people — including sheltering nuns and civilians — died beneath the rubble of its crypt and basement.
Holds the gravestones of Maria Karolina de Bouillon of the Sobieski family and Maria Józefa Sobieska.
w: A queen's votive church on the New Town square — Maria Kazimiera Sobieska's thanks for Vienna, a crowned dome by Tylman van Gameren of the 1690s. On the last day of August 1944 a bomb brought it down on the nuns and refugees in the crypt; around a thousand died beneath the rubble. Rebuilt, it stands quiet now. Beauty, and a mass grave.

hook: Warsaw's Dominican mother-church on Freta, hiding a domed two-storey Baroque tomb-chapel by Tylman van Gameren.
facts: The Dominicans bought the Freta plot in 1603; the first Mass in the new church was held on 22 October 1606 and the relics of St Hyacinth (św. Jacek) were brought to Warsaw.
Construction of the church was completed in 1638.
The Kotowski Chapel — a two-storey domed Baroque funerary chapel of Adam and Małgorzata Kotowski — was designed by Tylman van Gameren and built 1691–1694; its 18th-century furnishings survived WWII.
Marble sarcophagi of the Kotowski couple, benefactors of the church, lie in the crypt beneath the chapel.
A miraculous image of the Virgin of the Rosary, brought from Czortków in the eastern borderlands, was later placed in the chapel.
w: Warsaw's Dominican mother-church on Freta, finished in 1638 — and home to one Baroque marvel: the two-storey domed Kotowski Chapel, a funerary jewel by Tylman van Gameren whose 18th-century fittings survived the war. The Kotowskis lie in marble in the crypt below. The relics of St Hyacinth came to the city here. A tomb worth the visit.

hook: A pointed neo-Gothic Calvinist church that ended up wedged against the ghetto wall — Warsaw's tiny Reformed enclave made stone.
facts: Warsaw's Calvinists worshipped in secret from the 16th century until religious restrictions were lifted by the 1768 Warsaw treaty; they registered their Leszno parish in 1776.
The neo-Gothic church was designed by a parishioner, Adolf Loewe, built from 1866 and consecrated on 24 October 1880; construction took 14 years due to cost.
During WWII it stood in the 'evangelical enclave', surrounded on three sides by the ghetto wall, and was only partially damaged.
Houses 24-stop organs built in 1900 by Schlag und Söhne of Świdnica, plus original neo-Gothic woodwork including the font and pews.
Stands at al. Solidarności 74, formerly Leszno.
w: A spiky neo-Gothic church on the old Leszno, raised by Warsaw's tiny Calvinist minority and consecrated in 1880. In the war it stood in the 'evangelical enclave,' the ghetto wall on three sides, and survived only half-wrecked. Inside, a Silesian organ of 1900 and the original Gothic woodwork. The Reformed, made stone.

hook: Warsaw's oldest necropolis: a romantic city of the dead with brick catacombs and an Avenue of the Distinguished reading like a national who's-who.
facts: Founded 4 November 1790 on Szymanowski-family land, initially 2.6 ha; formally opened 20 May 1792 in the king's presence; around a million people have been buried here.
Early-19th-century brick catacombs near the oldest plots hold figures such as Hugo Kołłątaj and Michał Poniatowski.
The Aleja Zasłużonych (Avenue of the Distinguished) was created in 1925 along the catacomb wall, the first grave being that of Nobel laureate Władysław Reymont.
Among those on the Avenue: composer Stanisław Moniuszko, poets Bolesław Leśmian and Leopold Staff, writer Maria Dąbrowska, and singers Jan Kiepura and Kalina Jędrusik.
The Aleja Zasłużonych was entered into the register of historic monuments in 2020; distinct from the separate Military Powązki cemetery.
w: Warsaw's oldest cemetery, opened in 1792 — a romantic city of the dead with brick catacombs and an Avenue of the Distinguished that reads like a national roll-call: Reymont, Moniuszko, Leśmian, Staff, Kiepura. A million lie here under ivy and angels. Distinct from the military Powązki next door. Where Poland buries its great.

hook: A Lutheran necropolis laid out on a grid by the architect Zug — and resting place of the Wedel chocolate dynasty.
facts: Founded 2 May 1792 on land donated by Karol Schulz, on a 4.5 ha plot laid out by Szymon Bogumił Zug with a grid of numbered quarters; about 100,000 people are buried here.
The cemetery's landmark Halpert Chapel (Kaplica Halpertów) was built 1833–1835 to a design by Adolf Schuch, commissioned by Maria Halpertowa as a family mausoleum.
Among the buried: architect Szymon Bogumił Zug himself, lexicographer Samuel Bogumił Linde, painter Wojciech Gerson and industrialist Emil Wedel.
Holds the symbolic grave of Bishop Juliusz Bursche, murdered by the Nazis.
Days of heavy fighting crossed the cemetery during the 1944 Warsaw Uprising, causing major destruction.
w: A Lutheran cemetery of 1792, laid out by the architect Zug on a neat grid of numbered quarters — and the resting place of Warsaw's Protestant burghers: the lexicographer Linde, the painter Gerson, and Emil Wedel of the chocolate dynasty. The Halpert family's chapel is its landmark. Hard fighting crossed it in 1944. Old Warsaw, in rows.

hook: Poland's largest Orthodox necropolis, watched over by an onion-domed cerkiew built in grief for one man — and scarred by the Wola massacre.
facts: Established by tsarist decree in 1834 as Warsaw's Orthodox population grew; consecrated 1839 and ceremonially opened 1841, with the parish of St John Climacus founded in 1841.
The Cerkiew św. Jana Klimaka (1902–1905, consecrated 1 October 1905) was a private foundation of Archbishop Hieronim (Ekzemplarsky) commemorating his son Ivan, who died in 1902.
The church was designed by Vladimir Pokrovsky in a 17th-century Rostov style.
During the August 1944 Wola massacre the cemetery and church saw mass executions; almost the entire clergy, choir members and children from the parish orphanage were murdered.
The 13.3 ha cemetery is the largest Orthodox necropolis in Poland; in the monument register since 1985, with the church added in 2003.
w: Poland's largest Orthodox cemetery, opened under the Tsar in the 1840s — watched over by an onion-domed cerkiew an archbishop built for his dead son. In the Wola massacre of August 1944 the Germans murdered almost the whole parish: clergy, choir, orphanage children. Thirteen hectares of crosses on Wola. Grief, founded on grief.

hook: A stark white minimalist box dropped beside the Stalinist Palace of Culture — modern Warsaw staring down the old.
facts: Designed by New York studio Thomas Phifer and Partners with APA Wojciechowski, opened to the public on 25 October 2024.
Stands at Marszałkowska 103 on Plac Defilad, on the western edge of the square beside the Palace of Culture and Science.
Has roughly 19,788 m2 of floor area over four above-ground and two underground floors, with over 4,500 m2 of exhibition space.
Its white horizontal mass was deliberately designed to contrast with the steel-grey socialist-realist Palace of Culture.
Houses a cinema, auditorium, education rooms, conservation workshops and a cafe in addition to galleries.
legend: Architect Thomas Phifer said the design was meant to ensure the museum would not 'disappear' against the bulk of the Palace of Culture.
w: A stark white minimalist box dropped on Plac Defilad in 2024, deliberately set to stare down the grey Stalinist Palace of Culture beside it — Thomas Phifer's gallery for Warsaw's contemporary art. Four floors of clean light, a cinema and workshops within. New Poland, squaring up to the old. Modernism, answering back.

hook: The grand 'ARTIBUS' temple of art where Poland's first president was shot dead, days after taking office.
facts: Built 1898–1900 to a Renaissance-style design by Stefan Szyller, architect of the Warsaw Polytechnic main building, at Plac Małachowskiego 3.
The tympanum personifying the arts, bearing the inscription 'ARTIBUS' (For the Arts), was sculpted by Zygmunt Otto.
Grew out of the Society for the Encouragement of Fine Arts (Towarzystwo Zachęty Sztuk Pięknych), founded in 1860.
On 16 December 1922, Poland's first president Gabriel Narutowicz was assassinated inside the building by painter Eligiusz Niewiadomski.
A commemorative plaque marking the assassination was unveiled above the entrance to the Narutowicz Hall on 14 December 2000.
Today it is a national gallery presenting contemporary Polish and international art.
legend: Narutowicz was killed only five days into his presidency, shot while viewing an exhibition at Zachęta.
w: The grand 'ARTIBUS' temple of art off Plac Małachowskiego, Szyller's Renaissance pile of 1900 — and the place where Poland's first president was shot dead. Days into office, in December 1922, Gabriel Narutowicz was assassinated here by a painter as he viewed the pictures. A national gallery still. Art, and a murder, under one inscription.

hook: A 17th-century royal castle, rebuilt from wartime ruin and handed entirely to contemporary art.
facts: Stands at Jazdów 2 on the Ujazdów escarpment, on the site of the medieval Jazdów settlement whose history reaches back to the 11th–12th century.
Reconstruction of the war-ruined castle began in 1975 to a design by Piotr Biegański.
In 1981 the PRL authorities decided to dedicate the rebuilt castle to contemporary art.
The Centre for Contemporary Art was formally established on 18 July 1985, with programming from 1988.
Its dynamic development began after Wojciech Krukowski became director on 15 January 1990.
w: A 17th-century royal castle on the Ujazdów escarpment, burned in the war and left a ruin for decades — then rebuilt in the 1970s and handed, wholesale, to contemporary art. The Centre for Contemporary Art has filled it since the 1980s, defiantly experimental. A king's castle, turned over to the avant-garde. Old walls, new ideas.

hook: The world's largest hoard of Chopin relics, staged as a multimedia maze inside a baroque castle.
facts: Housed in the Ostrogski Castle (Pałac Gnińskich) at Okólnik 1, run by the National Fryderyk Chopin Institute.
A new multimedia permanent exhibition by Italian studio Migliore + Servetto opened on 1 March 2010, the 200th anniversary of Chopin's birth.
The exhibition spans twelve themes across four levels of the castle, including adapted basements.
The collection holds over 7,500 Chopin-related objects — the largest and most varied body of Chopiniana in the world.
The 2010 renovation and exhibition cost 81.5 million zloty, funded from the state budget and EU funds.
w: The largest hoard of Chopin relics on earth — 7,500 objects — staged as a multimedia maze across four floors of a baroque castle on Okólnik. The exhibition opened in 2010, on the composer's 200th birthday, themed and interactive. Manuscripts, letters, a lock of hair, his last piano. Everything Chopin, in the Ostrogski walls.

hook: A thousand years of Polish arms, finally given a home worthy of the collection inside the Citadel fortress.
facts: Founded in 1920 by decree of Marshal Józef Piłsudski, the museum long lacked its own dedicated building.
Its new Southern Pavilion on the Warsaw Citadel, designed by studio WXCA, opened ceremonially on 13 August 2023.
The inaugural exhibition '1000 Years of Glory of Polish Arms' presents some 3,000 objects across about 2,700 m2.
The full collection numbers over 300,000 artefacts — weapons, uniforms and military vehicles.
The building anchors the emerging Citadel of Museums complex alongside the Polish History Museum and Katyń Museum.
w: A thousand years of Polish arms — 3,000 of them on show, from a collection of 300,000 — finally housed in a building worthy of the hoard: WXCA's sharp new pavilion in the Citadel, opened in 2023. Swords, hussar wings, uniforms, tanks. The museum Piłsudski founded in 1920, homeless for a century, home at last.

n: Muzeum Azji i Pacyfiku im. Andrzeja Wawrzyniaka
hook: One diplomat's smuggled-home Indonesian collection grew into Poland's window on Asia and Oceania.
facts: Grew from the private Indonesian art and craft collection — about 3,000 objects — gathered by diplomat Andrzej Wawrzyniak in Jakarta between 1961 and 1971.
The collection was formally handed to the state on 26 February 1973, with Wawrzyniak as director until 2013.
Its Solec 24 site occupies twin late-classicist buildings of 1854 designed by Piotr Frydrych; the first was opened to use in 1983.
The museum today holds around 21,000 exhibits from almost every country of Asia and much of Oceania.
On 29 August 2017 the Mazovian regional assembly gave the museum Andrzej Wawrzyniak's name in his honour.
w: One diplomat's obsession, grown into Poland's window on Asia — Andrzej Wawrzyniak shipped home 3,000 Indonesian pieces from his Jakarta postings in the 1960s and gave them to the state. The collection now runs to 21,000 objects from across Asia and Oceania, in twin 1850s buildings on Solec. Masks, krises, shadow puppets.

hook: Sixty thousand relics of Polish sport, from ancient Olympia to the medal table, inside the Olympic Centre.
facts: Founded in the early 1950s, originally on Rozbrat Street; from 1966 at the Skra stadium on Wawelska Street.
Since 2005 it has occupied the Centrum Olimpijskie im. Jana Pawła II at Wybrzeże Gdyńskie 4 in Żoliborz.
It is the largest sports museum in Poland and one of the oldest in Europe.
Collections hold close to 60,000 exhibits, including over 18,000 photographs.
Its permanent display traces sport from ancient Greece and the 19th-century revival of the Olympics through Polish sporting history.
w: Sixty thousand relics of Polish sport, from ancient Olympia to the medal podium, in the Olympic Centre on the Vistula's Żoliborz bank. One of Europe's oldest sports museums, going back to the early 1950s. Medals, kit, eighteen thousand photographs, the whole arc from the Greek games to Polish glory. For anyone who loves a final whistle.

hook: Poland's biggest hoard of minerals and meteorites, split between an 18th-century pavilion and a former Masonic villa.
facts: Occupies two buildings on either side of Aleja Na Skarpie (20/26 and 27) on the Frascati escarpment.
The Biały Pałacyk na Frascati was designed by Szymon Bogumił Zug around 1781 as a summer residence for Prince Kazimierz Poniatowski.
The neighbouring Willa Pniewskiego — once linked to a Radziwiłł Masonic lodge and later a Branicki zoological museum — was redesigned by architect Bohdan Pniewski in 1935–1938.
The Society of the Museum of the Earth was founded in Warsaw in 1932 to create the museum.
Its collections number over 190,000 objects, including the largest collection of minerals and rocks in Poland.
w: Poland's biggest hoard of minerals and meteorites — 190,000 of them — split between Zug's white 1780s pavilion and a former Masonic villa on the Frascati escarpment. Crystals, amber, fossils, rocks from the bottom of deep time. Two graceful old houses on the slope above the river. The earth, catalogued and lit.

hook: The frozen pre-war flat of a writer, soldier and Masonic Grand Master, kept exactly as he left it.
facts: A branch of the Adam Mickiewicz Museum of Literature, set up in 1981 in the Strug family's pre-war apartment.
Located in flat 10A at Aleja Niepodległości 210, one of three buildings of the inter-war 'Ognisko' housing cooperative in Mokotów.
The biographical and literary display covers Andrzej Strug's prose and journalism alongside his political life.
Strug was a PPS activist, Polish Legions soldier and senator.
He served as Grand Master of the National Grand Lodge of Poland from 1922 to 1925.
w: The pre-war flat of Andrzej Strug — writer, PPS firebrand, Legions soldier and Masonic Grand Master — kept frozen exactly as he left it, in a 1920s Mokotów co-op. Books, papers, the furniture of a life that spanned literature and revolution. A small literary museum, and a sealed moment. One man's rooms, against the clock.

hook: A neo-Renaissance palace telling how a royal-election field became Warsaw's grimy industrial heart.
facts: A branch of Muzeum Warszawy, founded in 1974 on the initiative of the Wola branch of the Society of Friends of Warsaw.
Housed in the Pałacyk Sikorskiego at Srebrna 12, a neo-Renaissance building of 1880 built for sculptor Aleksander Sikorski.
The building has been a listed monument since 1965 and was once owned by industrialist Maurycy Borman.
Its permanent 'Warszawska Wola' exhibition traces the district from a royal-election village into an industrial quarter.
The palace was modernised between 2016 and 2019, including facade renovation and accessibility works.
w: A neo-Renaissance palace on grimy Srebrna, telling how Wola went from a field where kings were elected to Warsaw's smoke-stacked industrial heart. Built in 1880 for a sculptor, later an industrialist's house, now a branch of the Warsaw Museum. The story of a working district, in a little palace. Wola, explained.

hook: A tiny room off Foksal Street where the avant-garde defied the communist era under a theory of 'Place'.
facts: A non-commercial avant-garde gallery established in 1966 at Foksal 1/4.
Founded by critics Wiesław Borowski, Anka Ptaszkowska and Mariusz Tchorek with artists including Tadeusz Kantor, Henryk Stażewski, Edward Krasiński and Zbigniew Gostomski.
Its programme rested on the 1966 essay 'Introduction to the General Theory of Place', framing the gallery as a 'sudden gap' in utilitarian reality.
It managed to show avant-garde work throughout the communist period, closing only briefly 'for renovation' during martial law.
Many of its actions and demonstrations became landmarks of Polish art history.
w: A tiny room off Foksal where, from 1966, the avant-garde ran rings round the communist state — Kantor, Stażewski, Krasiński and friends, working under a half-serious 'General Theory of Place' that called the gallery a sudden gap in dull reality. It kept showing radical art right through the PRL. Small room, large defiance.

hook: Magda Gessler's gilded shrine to Polish tradition in a Renaissance Old Town townhouse
facts: Located at ul. Świętojańska 2, in the historic Renaissance 'Mansjonaria' townhouse (the 'dom nad Kanałem') beside the Royal Castle.
An authorial project of restaurateur and TV personality Magda Gessler, conceived to celebrate the multicultural diversity of Polish cuisine.
The interior is laid out across seven richly decorated dining rooms inside the Old Town tenement.
Serves classic Polish dishes presented as a refined whole rather than rustic plates.
legend: Magda Gessler is one of Poland's most recognisable culinary figures, host of the TV show 'Kuchenne rewolucje'.
w: Magda Gessler's gilded shrine to Polish cooking, across seven lavish rooms in a Renaissance townhouse by the Royal Castle. The cuisine of old Poland done as fine dining, not rustic plates — duck, pierogi and game under chandeliers. One of two Gessler institutions on the Old Town's doorstep. Polish tradition, turned up to gold.

hook: The Chmielna pączki shop Varsovians queue around the block for
facts: Located at ul. Chmielna 13 in central Warsaw.
A long-running family bakery, repeatedly cited as one of the best in the city for its pączki (Polish doughnuts).
Famous for its long customer queues on Chmielna; fans are pointed to its right-bank (Praga) branch to buy without waiting.
Its pączki are praised for very soft dough, generous fillings and creative flavours.
legend: Regularly held up in Warsaw food press as the rival benchmark to A. Blikle for the classic Warsaw pączek.
w: The pączki shop Varsovians queue around the block for — a family bakery on Chmielna whose Polish doughnuts, soft and overstuffed, are reckoned among the city's best. The line can be brutal; regulars know to slip over to the Praga branch and skip it. Especially mobbed on Fat Thursday. Worth the wait, or the detour.

hook: Praga-born confectioner from the old Wedel works, now run by the third Dynowski generation
facts: Founded in 1946 at the corner of Stalowa and Środkowa in Praga by Jan Dynowski, a chocolate master from the former Wedel factory.
Originally called 'Kremówka'; later renamed 'Lukullus' after the Roman general Lucullus.
Now run by the third generation of the Dynowski family, with this flagship pâtisserie at ul. Mokotowska 52A.
Signature pastries include a Limoncello tart, Grand Marnier cheesecake with dulce de leche and an outsized coffee meringue.
legend: Routinely listed among Warsaw's best and most elegant cukiernie in city food guides.
w: A Praga confectioner since 1946, founded by a chocolate master out of the old Wedel works and run now by the third Dynowski generation. It started as 'Kremówka,' renamed for the Roman glutton Lucullus — fittingly, given the limoncello tarts and outsized coffee meringues. The flagship sits on Mokotowska. Sweetness with a pedigree.

hook: Veneer-panelled multitap that helped make Warsaw a craft-beer city
facts: Multitap bar at ul. Nowogrodzka 25, in the city centre.
Pours over 20 rotating taps of Polish and international beer plus a deep bottle list and house beer cocktails.
Interior fitted with veneered panels and soft armchairs; opens a garden in summer.
Part of the Kufle i Kapsle group, with sibling bars on Nowy Świat and in Żoliborz.
legend: Named in Warsaw beer-trail guides among the bars that anchored the city's craft-beer scene.
w: A veneer-panelled multitap that helped turn Warsaw into a craft-beer city — twenty-odd rotating taps of Polish and world beer, a deep bottle list and beer cocktails, soft armchairs and a summer garden. One of the bars that started the scene, now with siblings across town. Pull up a chair and work through the taps.

hook: The 2013 multitap in the SARP pavilion that was a precursor of Warsaw craft beer
facts: Craft-beer bar in the Polish Architects' Association (SARP) pavilion at Foksal 2 / Nowy Świat 6/12.
Opened in 2013 and described as a precursor of the real craft-beer-bar wave in Warsaw.
Has 15 taps emphasising Polish craft breweries alongside beers from around the world.
Serves bar snacks and pizza alongside the beer.
legend: Cited on Warsaw beer trails as one of the city's pioneering multitaps.
w: A craft-beer bar in the modernist SARP architects' pavilion off Nowy Świat — fifteen taps, heavy on Polish breweries, that helped kick off Warsaw's beer revolution back in 2013. Pizza and snacks to soak it up, a terrace for the warm months. 'Miracles on a stick,' the name promises. An early temple of good beer.

hook: Warsaw's grand 1901 brick market hall, still selling fish, fruit and flowers
facts: One of the twin Mirów market halls at pl. Mirowski 1, built 1899–1901 to a design rooted in Stefan Szyller's plans.
On opening it was Warsaw's largest commercial building, trading mainly in fresh fish and vegetables.
Raised on the site of demolished 18th-century cavalry barracks of the Imperial Guard, which gave the halls their name.
Site of mass executions of civilians at the start of the 1944 Warsaw Uprising; the burnt hall was later rebuilt and entered into the monuments register in 1986.
legend: The Mirów halls and the open-air market around them remain a working everyday institution of central Warsaw.
w: The working twin of the two great Mirów market halls, built in brick around 1900 and still selling fish, fruit and flowers under its iron roof — Warsaw's largest commercial building when it opened. It stands on the ground of demolished Imperial Guard barracks, which named it; the Germans shot civilians here in 1944. Markets, then and now.

hook: Viennese-style café of the Paderewski-founded Hotel Bristol, open since 1901
facts: Café inside Hotel Bristol at ul. Krakowskie Przedmieście 42/44.
The hotel property was bought in 1895 by a company that included pianist and future prime minister Ignacy Jan Paderewski.
The café has welcomed guests in its Art Nouveau / Viennese-style interior since 1901.
Dessert recipes are said to have remained unchanged for years, including raspberry cream, florentines and cheesecakes baked in the hotel bakery.
legend: Picasso reportedly signed one of the café's plates during a visit; the café trades heavily on its Paderewski heritage.
w: The Viennese café of the Hotel Bristol — the grand hotel a young Paderewski helped buy in 1895 — pouring coffee under Art Nouveau plasterwork since 1901. The pastry recipes are said to have barely changed in a century: raspberry cream, florentines, cheesecake from the hotel's own bakery. Old Warsaw at its most genteel.

hook: Book-lined literary café between the university and the BUW, a Powiśle fixture since 2007
facts: Café at ul. Oboźna 3, midway between the University of Warsaw and its BUW library.
Opened in 2007 on the site of a former hydraulics shop.
Conceived around books to read over coffee; shelves of books and board games fill the interior.
Long a go-to breakfast spot on Powiśle and a host of cultural events including Museum Night.
legend: Treated in city guides as one of Powiśle's iconic literary cafés.
w: A book-lined café on Oboźna, wedged between the university and its library, where the shelves are stocked with novels and board games to dawdle over your coffee. Opened in 2007 in a former hydraulics shop, it has been Powiśle's favourite breakfast and a quiet cultural hub ever since. Read, play, linger.

hook: PRL-nostalgia shot-and-snack den where frozen vodka meets Polish comfort food
facts: Polish restaurant and vodka bar at ul. Świętokrzyska 14, named after a 'setka' (a 100 ml glass of spirits).
Styled on the nostalgic People's-Republic 'zakąski i przekąski' (shot-and-snack) bars, with a touch of restaurant polish.
Menu runs to żurek, tripe, krupnik, tartare, jellied pork, pork knuckle, bigos, pork chops and pierogi.
A two-level venue serving frozen vodka alongside the food.
legend: Cited as a leading example of Warsaw's revived PRL-style vodka-and-snack bar trend.
w: A two-level nod to the People's-Republic shot-and-snack bar, named for the setka — the 100-millilitre glass of vodka it pours straight from the freezer. The food is honest Polish comfort: żurek, tripe, tartare, jellied pork, bigos, pierogi. Nostalgia with a sharper kitchen than the originals had. Order a frozen shot and zakąski.

hook: Black-granite obelisk to the Home Army's fallen — and, for decades of communism, the only place in Warsaw where the Uprising could be mourned out loud.
facts: Unveiled 1 August 1946 in plot A26 of the Powązki Military Cemetery, the symbolic heart of a concentrated field of re-buried Uprising soldiers exhumed from across the city in 1945–47.
Designed by Helena Kłosowicz (pseudonym 'Monika'), herself a liaison runner and soldier of the Warsaw Uprising, whose project won the competition.

A black granite prism on a wide base with pink marble corners, topped by an urn-shaped block carved with stylised eagles; bronze inscriptions read 'Żołnierzom AK poległym za wolność', '1939–1944' and 'Gloria Victis'.
The name 'Gloria Victis' ('Glory to the Vanquished') is borrowed from Eliza Orzeszkowa's January-Uprising story, honouring those who fought for freedom regardless of the outcome.
Through the PRL years, when official commemorations were banned, Varsovians gathered here at 17:00 — Hour 'W' — every 1 August, a tradition that continues today.
In 2023 the monument was placed under the formal care of the Warsaw Uprising Museum.
sources: https://www.polskieradio.pl/399/7980/artykul/2836484 (plot A26, Kłosowicz, care transferred to Warsaw Uprising Museum)
w: A black granite obelisk to the Home Army's dead, at the heart of the military Powązki — and, under communism, the one place the Uprising could be mourned aloud. Helena Kłosowicz, a runner in the fighting, designed it in 1946; its name, 'Glory to the Vanquished,' honours the defeated. Every 1 August at five, the crowds still come.

hook: A lonely marble column on Plac Teatralny that everyone calls the 'Warsaw Meridian' — and that is, in fact, nothing of the sort.
facts: Erected in 1872 in front of the Jabłonowski Palace as a station for geodetic and magnetic measurements, fixing coordinates relayed from the triangulation point atop the town-hall tower.
It served the great triangulation survey of the 52nd-parallel arc carried out across the Russian Empire in the 19th century.
Its top plate once carried the geographic coordinates, the elevation above sea level, and engraved perpendicular lines marking the parallel and meridian directions toward the town-hall spire.

Despite the popular name, it has no connection to Warsaw's true zero meridian — that line runs through the transit instrument of the University's Astronomical Observatory at longitude 21°01'39.26" E, not through this column.
After restoration it was formally re-designated in 2022 as the 'Pomnik Geodezji Europejskiej' (Monument to European Geodesy).
sources: https://zdm.waw.pl/aktualnosci/oznaczymy-warszawski-poludnik-zerowy-na-moscie-pieszo-rowerowym/ (the actual Warsaw zero meridian vs. the column confusion)
w: A lonely marble column on Plac Teatralny that everyone calls the Warsaw Meridian — and that is nothing of the kind. Set up in 1872 as a survey station for the triangulation of the 52nd parallel, it never marked the city's true meridian — that runs through the university observatory. A geodetic relic, misnamed for 150 years.

hook: One of the last pharmacy neons still burning over an Ochota pavement — its tubes glowing the same blue they did in the PRL, even after a botched repaint turned the metal box red.
facts: Mounted on the pharmacy at ul. Częstochowska 11/15, it is one of very few surviving, still-lit Ochota neons left in situ.
The serif lettering and original colour scheme were typical of Warsaw pharmacy neons — fat serifed capitals on a white-faced sheet-metal box with blue sides.
During a 2014 renovation the metal housing was repainted entirely red, departing from the original white-and-blue, but the neon tubes were kept and still glow their original blue.
It is a stop on the 'Ochocka ścieżka neonowa', a local trail documenting Ochota's surviving neon heritage.
sources: https://ochota.um.warszawa.pl/-/apteka (Częstochowska 11/15, surviving lit neon, 2014 repaint, original blue tubes, Ochota neon trail)
w: One of the last pharmacy neons still burning over an Ochota pavement — fat serifed capitals that have glowed the same blue since the People's Republic. A 2014 repaint clumsily turned its white-and-blue box red, but the tubes kept their original colour. A stop on Ochota's neon trail. Cold-war light, still on.

hook: Three bronze sappers driving a bridge-pile into the Vistula itself — a war monument that stands not on land but in the river current at the mouth of the Czerniaków port.
facts: Unveiled 8 May 1975, on the 30th anniversary of the war's end, to honour the engineers who cleared mines and rubble from ruined Warsaw after January 1945.
Designed by sculptor Stanisław Kulon (with Eugeniusz Kozalek); it is a three-part work scattered across nearby sites in Park Rydza-Śmigłego by the Wisłostrada.
The river element — three soldiers ramming a reinforced-concrete pile into the bed at the entrance to the Czerniaków port — recalls the sappers' bridge-building on the Vistula.

The main land structure raises six 17-metre pylons symbolising a mine explosion, with a sapper figure defusing ordnance; an underground passage holds a bronze relief of Polish Army troops aiding the Uprising at Czerniaków in 1944.
Its inscription quotes a March 1945 army order: 'Free Warsaw will never forget those who through their labour and blood first began the work of its reconstruction.'
sources: https://www.polskaniezwykla.pl/web/place/28899 (Pomnik Saperów na Cyplu Czerniakowskim, three-part composition)
w: A war monument that stands not on land but in the Vistula itself — three bronze sappers ramming a bridge-pile into the riverbed at the mouth of the Czerniaków port. Unveiled in 1975 to the engineers who cleared the mines and rubble from ruined Warsaw and bridged this river under fire. Soldiers, working in the current.

n: Portal i drzwi ze śladami kul (Nowomiejska 7)
hook: A rebuilt Old Town tenement deliberately keeping its scars: an 18th-century stone portal and iron-clad doors still pocked with 1944 bullet holes.
facts: The building at Nowomiejska 7 collapsed during the Warsaw Uprising, probably on 19 August 1944, and was rebuilt in 1954–55.
During reconstruction the surviving original stone portal (18th century) and its decorative iron-clad doors were deliberately reused as 'priceless testimony' to the fighting on the Old Town.
The doors date to 1742 and were not original to this house — they were brought over from ul. Piwna 35, ornamented with ribbons, rosettes and palmettes and studded with nails.
Bullet and shrapnel marks from the 1944 combat remain visible on both the portal and the doors and were knowingly left unrepaired.
It is one of the stops in the 'Ślady Kul, Ślady Pamięci' (Bullet Marks, Memory Marks) documentation project.
sources: https://sladypamieci.waw.pl/nowomiejska-7/ (1742 doors from Piwna 35, 18th-c. portal, bullet marks deliberately preserved in 1954–55 rebuild)
w: An Old Town tenement on Nowomiejska, rebuilt after the war — and made to keep its scars. Its 18th-century stone portal and iron-clad doors of 1742 are still pocked with bullet holes from 1944, left unrepaired as testimony. Most of the Old Town was healed seamlessly; this doorway was not. The war, left visible.

hook: A worn stone beast crouching by the Old Town cathedral — said to be an enchanted prince waiting for a maiden's kiss, though zoologists insist she's actually a lioness.
facts: The carved figure sits on ul. Świętojańska beside the church of St John, in the heart of the Old Town.
It was sculpted in the mid-18th century by Jan Jerzy Plersch, originally as one of a pair (a bear and a lion); it migrated to Świętojańska in the 19th century.
One of the two figures was destroyed in the Warsaw Uprising in 1944.
By its tail the surviving animal is reckoned to be a lioness, even though tradition has always known it as 'the bear'.
legend: Legend says the figure is an enchanted prince — a kind, noble nobleman whose face resembled a bear's, who turned fully to stone the moment he fell hopelessly in love with a beautiful girl glimpsed at Mass in the Jesuit church.
He is to regain human form only if a maiden falls in love with him and kisses the statue.
sources: https://warszawawpigulce.pl/legendy-warszawskie-kamienny-niedzwiedz/ (mid-18th c., Jan Jerzy Plersch, bear+lion pair, one lost in 1944, prince legend, actually a lioness)
https://www.suerteprzewodnicy.pl/kamienny-niedzwiedz-z-ulicy-swietojanskiej-legendy-warszawskie/ (location on Świętojańska by St John's, legend of the kiss)
w: A worn stone beast crouching by the Old Town cathedral on Świętojańska, known to all as 'the bear' — and, by the story, an enchanted prince awaiting a maiden's kiss. Plersch carved it in the 18th century, one of a pair until 1944 took the other. Look at the tail, though: zoologists say she's a lioness. Believe the legend instead.

hook: A sun-and-beach mosaic glittering behind a Mokotów shopfront — the last trace of a 1960s 'spa' where Varsovians sipped Konstancin mineral water without leaving the city.
facts: The ground-floor premises at ul. Puławska 51 once housed a mineral-water drinking hall (pijalnia wód) of the Konstancin health resort, set up in the 1960s.
Its rounded interior walls and a structural column are clad in characteristic 1950s–60s mosaic of ceramic tiles and raw pebbles, evoking a beach and sun.
Authorship is attributed (unconfirmed) to Krzysztof Henisz.
After the spa closed, the space served other uses (latterly a bakery shop) before standing empty and being offered for rent by the city.
The interior decoration was entered into the register of monuments as a movable cultural property — a rare example of preserved PRL-era decorative interior art.
sources: https://www.whitemad.pl/mozaika-dawnej-pijalni-wod-mineralnych-wpisana-do-rejestru-zabytkow/ (Konstancin pijalnia wód, 1960s, sun/beach mosaic, Henisz attribution, monument listing)
https://www.warszawskie-mozaiki.pl/2023/04/mozaika-puawska-51.html (Puławska 51, 1960s mosaic, ceramic and pebble, monument register)
w: Behind a Mokotów shopfront, a sun-and-beach mosaic of ceramic tiles and raw pebbles glitters on curved walls — the last trace of a 1960s drinking hall where Varsovians sipped Konstancin spa water without leaving town. The spa closed long ago; a bakery came and went; the city listed the mosaic as heritage. PRL leisure, fossilised in tile.

hook: Hidden in the courtyard of the Czapski Palace, a 19th-century cast-iron street pump whose spout ends in a snarling dragon's head — a survivor of Warsaw's pre-waterworks days.
facts: The pump stands in the courtyard of the Pałac Czapskich, today the Academy of Fine Arts (ASP), behind the gate at Krakowskie Przedmieście 5.
It is a 19th-century cast-iron water pump whose tap terminates in a sculpted dragon's (smok) head.
It was made at the Troetzer & Emmel 'Fabryka Maszyn i Urządzeń' (Machine and Equipment Factory), one of Warsaw's notable 19th–20th-century manufacturers.
It is a remnant of the era when Varsovians drew water from public wells and street pumps before the Lindley municipal waterworks.
sources: https://ulicetwojegomiasta.pl/ulica/krakowskie-przedmiescie (cast-iron pump with dragon-head spout, Troetzer & Emmel, 19th c., courtyard of Pałac Czapskich/ASP, Krakowskie Przedmieście 5)
w: In the courtyard of the Czapski Palace — now the Academy of Fine Arts — a cast-iron street pump still stands, its spout a snarling dragon's head. It survives from the days before the Lindley waterworks, when the city drew water from public wells. Slip through the gate on Krakowskie Przedmieście and find the dragon.
